grammar_points.name: Present Perfect
grammar_points.summary: Diễn tả hành động đã xảy ra và có liên hệ với hiện tại, hoặc trong khoảng thời gian chưa kết thúc.

grammar_points.name: Passive Voice
grammar_points.summary: Dùng khi muốn nhấn mạnh đối tượng chịu tác động thay vì chủ thể thực hiện hành động.

grammar_points.name: Conditional Sentences
grammar_points.summary: Diễn tả điều kiện và kết quả. Type 1 (có thể xảy ra), Type 2 (giả định), Type 3 (không thể xảy ra).

grammar_points.name: Relative Clauses
grammar_points.summary: Dùng để bổ nghĩa cho danh từ. Hai loại: defining (xác định) và non-defining (không xác định).

grammar_points.name: Modal Verbs
grammar_points.summary: Diễn tả khả năng, sự cho phép, nghĩa vụ, lời khuyên. Gồm: can, could, may, might, must, should, would.

grammar_points.name: Reported Speech
grammar_points.summary: Dùng để thuật lại lời nói của người khác. Thì động từ lùi về quá khứ.

grammar_points.name: Articles (a/an/the)
grammar_points.summary: a/an dùng cho danh từ đếm được số ít lần đầu nhắc đến. the dùng khi đã xác định hoặc duy nhất.

grammar_points.name: Gerunds & Infinitives
grammar_points.vietnamese_name: Danh động từ và động từ nguyên mẫu
grammar_points.summary: Gerund (V-ing) và infinitive (to + V) dùng sau một số động từ nhất định. Một số động từ dùng được cả hai nhưng nghĩa khác nhau.

grammar_points.name: Comparison
grammar_points.summary: So sánh hơn (comparative), so sánh nhất (superlative), so sánh bằng (as...as). Dùng nhiều trong writing và speaking.

grammar_points.name: Conjunctions & Linking Words
grammar_points.summary: Dùng để kết nối ý trong câu và đoạn văn. Quan trọng cho writing: addition, contrast, cause/effect, concession.

grammar_structures.description: Đã xác định hoặc duy nhất
Động từ theo sau bởi gerund
Động từ theo sau bởi infinitive

grammar_structures.template: Addition: furthermore, moreover, in addition, besides
Contrast: however, nevertheless, on the other hand, although
Cause/Effect: therefore, consequently, as a result, thus
Concession: although, even though, despite, in spite of

grammar_common_mistakes.wrong: The letter was wrote by her.
grammar_common_mistakes.correct: The letter was written by her.

grammar_common_mistakes.wrong: If I will go, I will call you.
grammar_common_mistakes.correct: If I go, I will call you.
grammar_common_mistakes.explanation: Mệnh đề if không dùng will.

grammar_common_mistakes.wrong: If he studied, he will pass.
grammar_common_mistakes.correct: If he studied, he would pass.

grammar_common_mistakes.wrong: The man which called you is here.
grammar_common_mistakes.correct: The man who called you is here.
grammar_common_mistakes.explanation: Dùng who cho người, which cho vật.

grammar_common_mistakes.wrong: She is the girl whose her bag was stolen.
grammar_common_mistakes.correct: She is the girl whose bag was stolen.
grammar_common_mistakes.explanation: Sau whose không dùng thêm her/his.
Modal verbs không dùng to trước động từ.
Sau modal dùng bare infinitive, không chia.

grammar_common_mistakes.wrong: He asked where do I live.
grammar_common_mistakes.explanation: Câu hỏi gián tiếp dùng trật tự câu khẳng định.
Dùng an trước âm nguyên âm (h câm).

grammar_common_mistakes.wrong: I go to the school every day.
grammar_common_mistakes.correct: I go to school every day.
grammar_common_mistakes.explanation: Không dùng the với school/work/church khi nói về mục đích.
enjoy + V-ing, không dùng to + V.

grammar_common_mistakes.wrong: She is more taller than her sister.
grammar_common_mistakes.correct: She is taller than her sister.
grammar_common_mistakes.explanation: Không dùng more với tính từ ngắn.

grammar_common_mistakes.wrong: He is the most tallest student.
grammar_common_mistakes.explanation: Không dùng most với tính từ ngắn.

grammar_common_mistakes.wrong: Although it was cold, but she went out.
grammar_common_mistakes.correct: Although it was cold, she went out.
grammar_common_mistakes.explanation: Không dùng although và but cùng lúc.

grammar_common_mistakes.wrong: Despite of the rain, we went out.
grammar_common_mistakes.correct: Despite the rain, we went out.
grammar_common_mistakes.explanation: Despite không dùng of, dùng in spite of nếu muốn.

grammar_vstep_tips.tip: Dùng present perfect để nêu kinh nghiệm trong essay.
grammar_vstep_tips.example: Many students have experienced online learning.

grammar_vstep_tips.tip: Dùng bị động để viết văn học thuật, tránh dùng I quá nhiều.
grammar_vstep_tips.example: It has been argued that technology improves education.

grammar_vstep_tips.tip: Dùng type 2 để đề xuất giải pháp giả định trong essay.
grammar_vstep_tips.example: If governments invested more in education, poverty rates would decrease significantly.

grammar_vstep_tips.tip: Dùng relative clauses để viết câu phức tạp hơn, tránh lặp từ.
grammar_vstep_tips.example: Technology, which has transformed education, offers new learning opportunities.

grammar_vstep_tips.tip: Dùng should/could/might để đề xuất giải pháp trong essay.
grammar_vstep_tips.example: Governments should invest more in public transport to reduce congestion.

grammar_vstep_tips.tip: Dùng reported speech khi viết thư tường thuật lại cuộc trò chuyện.
grammar_vstep_tips.example: My friend told me that the event had been cancelled.

grammar_vstep_tips.tip: Dùng the khi nhắc lại khái niệm đã giới thiệu trong đoạn trước.
grammar_vstep_tips.example: Technology has changed education. The impact of technology is undeniable.

grammar_vstep_tips.tip: Dùng gerund sau preposition để viết câu đa dạng hơn.
grammar_vstep_tips.example: By investing in education, governments can reduce poverty.

grammar_vstep_tips.tip: Dùng comparative để so sánh hai quan điểm trong essay.
grammar_vstep_tips.example: Online learning is more flexible than traditional classroom learning.

grammar_vstep_tips.tip: Dùng đa dạng linking words để tăng điểm coherence trong essay.
grammar_vstep_tips.example: Furthermore, governments should invest in renewable energy. Nevertheless, this requires significant funding.

grammar_exercises.payload: {"prompt":"They decided ___ a new house.","options":["buying","buy","to buy","bought"],"correct_index":2}

exam_version_listening_sections.transcript:
Receptionist: Good afternoon, Grand Palace Hotel. How may I help you?
Guest: Hello, I'd like to book a room for three nights, from the 15th to the 18th of next month.
Receptionist: Let me check availability. For those dates, we have a double room available on the 8th floor with a city view.
Guest: That sounds nice. What's the rate?
Receptionist: It's 1.2 million dong per night, and that includes breakfast for two.
Guest: Does it include anything else, like airport transfer?
Receptionist: I'm afraid the airport transfer is an additional charge. But breakfast is included in the room rate.
Guest: Alright, I'll take it.
Receptionist: Wonderful. Could I have your passport number to complete the reservation?
Guest: Sure, let me get it.

Dear students, please be informed that the university library will have adjusted hours this week due to maintenance work. From Monday to Friday, the library will close at 8 PM instead of the usual 10 PM. Regular hours will resume next Monday. We apologize for any inconvenience.

Dear colleagues, the IT department will be performing system maintenance this Saturday from 8 AM to 2 PM. During this time, access to the company email and internal systems will be unavailable. Please save all important work before Friday evening.

Doctor: Good morning. What seems to be the problem today?
Patient: I have had a terrible headache for three days and I feel dizzy sometimes.
Doctor: I see. Have you been sleeping well?
Patient: Not really. I have been working very long hours this week.
Doctor: That could be the cause. I would like to check your blood pressure. It seems slightly elevated. I am going to prescribe some medication and I strongly recommend you take a few days off work.
Patient: Is it serious?
Doctor: Not at this stage, but if you continue at this pace, it could become a problem. I would also suggest reducing your caffeine intake and drinking more water.
Patient: How long should I take the medication?
Doctor: For one week. Come back and see me if the headaches do not improve.
Attention gym members. The swimming pool will be closed for maintenance from Monday to Wednesday next week. The gym and fitness classes will remain open as usual. We apologize for any inconvenience.

Passengers are advised that due to road construction on Le Loi Street, bus routes 12 and 15 will be diverted via Nguyen Trai Street from Monday. The diversion is expected to last approximately three weeks.
Dear students, the university library will extend its opening hours during the examination period. From next Monday, the library will be open from 7 AM to midnight, seven days a week.
Good evening, diners. This month, every table of four or more receives a complimentary dessert platter. Simply mention this offer when ordering. The promotion runs until the end of the month.

Welcome to today lecture on urban farming, a growing movement that is transforming how cities think about food production. Urban farming refers to the practice of growing food within city boundaries, using spaces such as rooftops, balconies, vacant lots, and even indoor vertical farms.

The movement has gained momentum for several reasons. First, it addresses food security concerns in densely populated cities. By producing food locally, cities can reduce their dependence on long supply chains that are vulnerable to disruption. During the COVID-19 pandemic, for example, many urban farms played a crucial role in supplying fresh produce when distribution networks were strained.

Second, urban farming has environmental benefits. It reduces the carbon footprint associated with transporting food over long distances. Green roofs and vertical gardens also help regulate building temperatures, reducing energy consumption for heating and cooling.

Third, urban farms create community spaces. Community gardens bring residents together, provide educational opportunities for children, and can improve mental health by connecting people with nature.

However, urban farming faces significant challenges. Land in cities is expensive and scarce. Water usage can be high. And the scale of production is generally too small to feed entire urban populations. Urban farming is best understood as a supplement to, rather than a replacement for, conventional agriculture.

Despite these limitations, the future of urban farming looks promising. Advances in vertical farming technology, hydroponics, and LED lighting are making indoor food production increasingly efficient and cost-effective.

Good morning, everyone. Today I want to discuss the effects of global warming on our planet. As you may know, the global average temperature has increased by approximately 1.1 degrees Celsius since the pre-industrial era. This may seem like a small number, but its effects are enormous. One of the most visible consequences is the melting of polar ice caps. The Arctic ice sheet has been shrinking at an alarming rate, losing about 13 percent per decade. This contributes to rising sea levels, which threaten coastal communities worldwide. Scientists warn that if we don't take urgent action to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, we could see a temperature increase of 3 degrees or more by the end of this century. The situation is urgent, and we must act now. Reducing carbon emissions through renewable energy, better transportation, and changes in consumption patterns are all essential steps.

Let's take a journey through the history of the internet. It all began in 1969 when the United States Department of Defense created ARPANET, a network designed for military communication during the Cold War. The original purpose was to create a communication system that could survive a nuclear attack. For the first two decades, the internet was mainly used by researchers and academics. The real transformation came in 1991 when Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web, making the internet accessible to ordinary people. Since then, the growth has been extraordinary. Today, there are approximately 5 billion internet users worldwide. However, this rapid growth has also raised serious concerns, particularly about privacy. Our personal data is constantly being collected, stored, and sometimes misused by companies and governments.

Thank you for joining today's health seminar. I'd like to talk about the importance of maintaining a balanced diet. Many people underestimate how much their food choices affect their overall health. Nutritionists recommend eating at least five servings of fruits and vegetables every day. These provide essential vitamins, minerals, and fiber that our bodies need. Speaking of fiber, this is the nutrient I want to focus on today. Fiber helps with digestion, reduces cholesterol, and can even help prevent certain types of cancer. Unfortunately, many people rely too heavily on processed food. While it may be convenient, processed food should be avoided as much as possible because it's often high in sugar, salt, and unhealthy fats. My recommendation is simple: start keeping a food diary. Write down everything you eat for two weeks. You'll be surprised at the patterns you discover, and it will help you make better choices.

Manager: That is completely reasonable. Let us schedule a team meeting next week to establish clearer guidelines for project attribution.

Good morning. Today I want to explore what psychological research tells us about happiness and well-being. For much of history, happiness was considered a philosophical concept. Today, it is a serious field of scientific inquiry.

Researchers distinguish between two types of happiness. Hedonic happiness refers to the experience of pleasure and the absence of pain. Eudaimonic happiness refers to a deeper sense of meaning, purpose, and fulfillment. Studies suggest that eudaimonic happiness is more strongly associated with long-term well-being.

One of the most robust findings in happiness research is the importance of social connections. People with strong relationships consistently report higher levels of happiness than those who are socially isolated. The Harvard Study of Adult Development, which followed participants for over 80 years, found that the quality of relationships was the single strongest predictor of happiness and health in old age.

Money does have some relationship with happiness, but the effect is more limited than most people assume. Research suggests that happiness increases with income up to a point where basic needs and security are met, but beyond that, additional wealth has diminishing returns on well-being.

Gratitude practices have been shown to significantly boost happiness. People who regularly write down things they are grateful for report higher levels of positive emotion and life satisfaction. Exercise is another powerful happiness booster, with studies showing effects comparable to antidepressant medication for mild to moderate depression.

The good news is that happiness is not entirely determined by genetics or circumstances. Research suggests that approximately 40% of our happiness is within our control through intentional activities and mindset.

Interviewer: Thank you for joining us today. Can you tell us about your community environmental project?
Activist: Of course. We started the Green Neighborhood Initiative three years ago. Our main goal is to reduce plastic waste in our local area.
Interviewer: What specific actions have you taken?
Activist: We have organized monthly clean-up events, installed recycling stations in the neighborhood, and run educational workshops in local schools.
Interviewer: What has been the biggest challenge?
Activist: Getting people to change their habits. Many residents understand the problem intellectually but find it difficult to change their daily routines.
Interviewer: Have you seen measurable results?
Activist: Yes, we have reduced plastic waste in our area by approximately 40% over three years. We have also inspired three neighboring communities to start similar initiatives.
Interviewer: What are your plans for the future?
Activist: We want to expand our school program and work with local businesses to eliminate single-use plastics entirely.

Good afternoon, everyone. Today I want to discuss the cognitive and social benefits of being bilingual. Research over the past two decades has consistently shown that speaking two languages provides significant advantages beyond simple communication.

First, bilingual individuals show enhanced executive function. This refers to the ability to focus attention, switch between tasks, and ignore irrelevant information. Because bilinguals constantly manage two language systems, their brains become more efficient at these cognitive control tasks.

Second, studies suggest that bilingualism may delay the onset of dementia by an average of four to five years. The mental exercise of managing two languages appears to build cognitive reserve, which helps the brain compensate for age-related decline.

Third, bilingual children often demonstrate greater cultural awareness and empathy. Exposure to two languages means exposure to two cultures, which broadens perspective and improves social skills.

However, it is important to note that bilingualism does not make children smarter in all areas. Academic performance depends on many factors including teaching quality, socioeconomic background, and individual motivation. The cognitive benefits are specific to executive function and certain memory tasks.

In conclusion, the evidence strongly supports encouraging bilingual education. The benefits extend beyond language skills to include cognitive health, cultural understanding, and social development.

Welcome to this lecture on the global transition to renewable energy. The shift away from fossil fuels toward clean energy sources is one of the defining challenges and opportunities of our time.

The case for renewable energy is compelling. Fossil fuels are finite resources that will eventually be exhausted. More urgently, burning them releases carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases that are driving climate change. The scientific consensus is clear: to limit global warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels, global carbon emissions must reach net zero by 2050.

Solar and wind power have experienced dramatic cost reductions over the past decade. The cost of solar electricity has fallen by approximately 90% since 2010, making it now the cheapest source of electricity in history in many parts of the world. Wind power has seen similar cost reductions.

However, the transition faces significant challenges. Renewable energy sources are intermittent: the sun does not always shine and the wind does not always blow. This requires investment in energy storage technologies, particularly batteries, and in grid infrastructure to balance supply and demand.

The transition also has social dimensions. Communities that depend on coal mining and fossil fuel industries face economic disruption. A just transition requires investment in retraining workers and developing alternative economic opportunities in affected regions.

Despite these challenges, the momentum behind renewable energy is accelerating. Many countries have set ambitious targets for carbon neutrality. Investment in clean energy now exceeds investment in fossil fuels globally. The question is no longer whether the transition will happen, but how quickly and equitably it can be achieved.

Today I want to take you through the fascinating history of money, from ancient barter systems to modern digital currencies. Understanding how money evolved helps us appreciate its role in society and economy.

Before money existed, people used barter, exchanging goods and services directly. While barter worked in small communities, it had a fundamental problem: the double coincidence of wants. You needed to find someone who had what you wanted and wanted what you had. This was inefficient and limited trade.

The solution was commodity money, where objects with intrinsic value, such as grain, cattle, or shells, were used as a medium of exchange. Around 600 BCE, the Lydians in modern-day Turkey are credited with minting the first metal coins. These coins had a standardized weight and were stamped with official marks to guarantee their value.

Paper money originated in China during the Tang Dynasty around 700 CE. Merchants found it inconvenient to carry heavy coins, so they deposited them with trusted agents and received paper receipts. These receipts gradually became accepted as payment themselves.

The gold standard, adopted by many countries in the 19th century, tied the value of paper money to a fixed amount of gold. This provided stability but limited governments ability to respond to economic crises. Most countries abandoned the gold standard during the 20th century.

Today, we use fiat money, which has value because governments declare it legal tender, not because it is backed by a physical commodity. The latest development is digital currency, including cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin, which operate without central bank control.

Today I want to discuss one of the most fascinating questions in linguistics: how did human language evolve? Language is uniquely human. While other animals communicate, no other species has developed a system of communication with the complexity, flexibility, and expressive power of human language.

The origins of language are difficult to study because language leaves no direct fossil record. Researchers must rely on indirect evidence, including the anatomy of ancient human skulls, the development of symbolic behavior in the archaeological record, and comparisons with other primates.

One influential theory is that language evolved gradually from earlier forms of communication. Our ancestors may have begun with simple calls and gestures, gradually developing more complex vocalizations. The development of the hyoid bone, which supports the tongue and enables complex speech sounds, is an important anatomical marker. Modern-shaped hyoid bones have been found in Neanderthal fossils, suggesting they may have had some capacity for speech.

Another theory proposes that language emerged relatively suddenly as a result of a genetic mutation that rewired the brain for symbolic thinking. This view is supported by the apparent explosion of symbolic behavior in the archaeological record around 50,000 years ago, including cave paintings, jewelry, and complex tools.

Language is not just a communication tool. It shapes how we think. The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis proposes that the language we speak influences our perception of reality. While the strong version of this hypothesis has been largely rejected, research does show that language affects certain aspects of cognition, including color perception and spatial reasoning.

Understanding language evolution helps us understand what makes us human. Language enables culture, cooperation, and the accumulation of knowledge across generations.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: Sleep Science and Modern Life
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
Sleep is one of the most fundamental biological needs, yet it is increasingly neglected in modern society. Research indicates that approximately one-third of adults in developed countries regularly fail to get the recommended seven to nine hours of sleep per night. The consequences of this widespread sleep deprivation are far-reaching and serious.

Sleep serves multiple critical functions. During sleep, the brain consolidates memories, processing and storing information acquired during waking hours. The body repairs tissues, synthesizes proteins, and releases growth hormones. The immune system is strengthened, and metabolic processes are regulated. Chronic sleep deprivation disrupts all of these functions.

The cognitive effects of insufficient sleep are well-documented. Sleep-deprived individuals show impaired attention, slower reaction times, and reduced ability to make complex decisions. Studies have found that being awake for 17 hours produces cognitive impairment equivalent to a blood alcohol level of 0.05%, and 24 hours of wakefulness is equivalent to 0.10%.

The health consequences extend beyond cognition. Chronic sleep deprivation is associated with increased risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and depression. The relationship between sleep and weight is particularly well-established: sleep deprivation disrupts hormones that regulate appetite, leading to increased hunger and preference for high-calorie foods.

Modern technology is a significant contributor to sleep problems. The blue light emitted by smartphones, tablets, and computers suppresses melatonin production, the hormone that signals the body to prepare for sleep. Social media and streaming services provide endless entertainment that competes with sleep time.

Addressing the sleep crisis requires both individual and societal changes. Individuals can improve sleep hygiene by maintaining consistent sleep schedules, creating dark and cool sleeping environments, and limiting screen time before bed. At a societal level, schools and workplaces could adjust their schedules to better align with natural sleep patterns.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: Community Libraries
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
Community libraries play an important role in modern society. They provide free access to books, computers, and the internet for people of all ages. Many libraries also offer educational programs such as reading clubs, language classes, and workshops for children and adults.

In recent years, libraries have changed significantly. They are no longer just places to borrow books. Today, many libraries have quiet study areas, meeting rooms, and even cafes. Some libraries lend unusual items such as tools, musical instruments, and board games.

Despite the rise of digital technology, libraries remain popular. Studies show that library visits have increased in many countries. People visit libraries not only to borrow books but also to use computers, attend events, and meet other community members.

Libraries also support lifelong learning. They help children develop reading habits from an early age. For adults, libraries provide resources for career development, including job search assistance and free online courses. Senior citizens benefit from large-print books and audiobooks.

Funding is a major challenge for libraries. Many rely on government support, which can be reduced during economic downturns. Some libraries have introduced membership fees or fundraising events to supplement their income. Community support is essential for keeping libraries open and well-stocked.

In conclusion, libraries are vital community resources. They promote education, reduce inequality, and bring people together. Investing in libraries is investing in the future of society.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: The Neuroscience of Learning
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
Recent advances in neuroscience have transformed our understanding of how the brain learns and how this knowledge can be applied to improve education. The brain is not a fixed organ but a dynamic system that changes in response to experience, a property known as neuroplasticity.

Learning occurs when neurons form new connections or strengthen existing ones. This process, called synaptic plasticity, is the cellular basis of memory and learning. The more frequently a neural pathway is activated, the stronger it becomes, which is why practice and repetition are fundamental to skill acquisition.

Spaced repetition is one of the most well-supported learning strategies in cognitive science. Rather than studying material in one long session, spacing practice over time leads to significantly better long-term retention. This is because each time we retrieve information from memory, we strengthen the neural pathways associated with that information.

The testing effect, also known as retrieval practice, is another powerful learning strategy. Research consistently shows that testing yourself on material, rather than simply re-reading it, leads to much better retention. This is because the act of retrieval itself strengthens memory, even when the retrieval is unsuccessful.

Sleep plays a crucial role in learning and memory consolidation. During sleep, the brain replays and consolidates memories formed during waking hours. Studies show that students who sleep after studying retain significantly more information than those who stay awake. Chronic sleep deprivation impairs the formation of new memories.

Emotional states also influence learning. Moderate levels of stress can enhance attention and memory formation, but chronic stress impairs cognitive function. Positive emotions and a sense of safety promote the kind of open, exploratory thinking that facilitates deep learning. This has important implications for classroom environments and teaching practices.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: The Rise of Remote Work
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated a trend that had been growing for years: remote work. Millions of employees worldwide shifted to working from home almost overnight. Companies that had resisted flexible working arrangements were forced to adapt quickly.

Remote work offers several advantages. Employees save time and money on commuting. They often report higher job satisfaction and better work-life balance. For companies, remote work can reduce office costs and allow them to hire talent from anywhere in the world.

However, remote work also presents challenges. Many workers struggle with isolation and the blurring of boundaries between work and personal life. Collaboration can be more difficult when team members are in different locations or time zones. Some employees, particularly those in small homes or with young children, find it hard to concentrate.

Technology has been crucial in enabling remote work. Video conferencing tools, project management software, and cloud storage have made it possible for teams to work effectively from different locations. Cybersecurity has become a greater concern as more sensitive data is accessed from home networks.

Not all jobs can be done remotely. Workers in manufacturing, healthcare, and retail must be physically present. This has highlighted existing inequalities in the workforce, as higher-paid knowledge workers enjoy the flexibility of remote work while lower-paid workers do not.

Many companies are now adopting hybrid models, combining office and remote work. This approach aims to balance the benefits of both arrangements. The future of work is likely to be more flexible than before the pandemic, though the exact shape of this flexibility is still being determined.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: Plastic Pollution and Ocean Health
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
Plastic pollution has become one of the most pressing environmental challenges of the 21st century. Every year, approximately 8 million tonnes of plastic waste enter the world oceans, threatening marine ecosystems and the species that depend on them.

Plastic does not biodegrade in the traditional sense. Instead, it breaks down into smaller and smaller pieces called microplastics, which can persist in the environment for hundreds of years. These tiny particles have been found in the deepest ocean trenches, in Arctic ice, and even in human blood.

Marine animals are particularly vulnerable to plastic pollution. Sea turtles mistake plastic bags for jellyfish and consume them, leading to internal blockages and death. Seabirds feed plastic fragments to their chicks, mistaking them for food. Whales have been found with hundreds of kilograms of plastic in their stomachs.

The economic impact is also significant. Fishing industries suffer as plastic entangles nets and contaminates catches. Tourism declines in areas with polluted beaches. The cost of cleaning up plastic pollution runs into billions of dollars annually.

Addressing plastic pollution requires action at multiple levels. Governments have introduced bans on single-use plastics such as straws, bags, and cutlery. Some countries have implemented extended producer responsibility schemes, requiring manufacturers to fund recycling programs. Consumer behavior change is also essential.

Innovation offers some hope. Biodegradable plastics, improved recycling technologies, and ocean cleanup projects are being developed. However, experts warn that these solutions alone are insufficient. Reducing plastic production at the source remains the most effective long-term strategy.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: The Psychology of Decision-Making
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
Human decision-making is far more complex than classical economic theory suggests. For decades, economists assumed that people make rational choices based on complete information and self-interest. However, research in behavioral economics and cognitive psychology has revealed that human judgment is systematically biased in predictable ways.

One of the most influential concepts is cognitive bias, introduced by psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky. Their research demonstrated that people rely on mental shortcuts, or heuristics, when making decisions. While these shortcuts are often useful, they can lead to systematic errors. For example, the availability heuristic causes people to overestimate the likelihood of events that come easily to mind, such as plane crashes, while underestimating more common but less memorable risks.

Another significant bias is loss aversion. Studies show that people feel the pain of losing something approximately twice as strongly as the pleasure of gaining something of equal value. This asymmetry influences financial decisions, negotiations, and even political behavior. Investors, for instance, often hold onto losing stocks too long because selling would mean acknowledging a loss.

The framing effect demonstrates that the way information is presented significantly influences choices. People respond differently to a medical treatment described as having a 90% survival rate versus one described as having a 10% mortality rate, even though these are mathematically identical.

Nudge theory, developed by Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein, applies these insights to policy design. By changing the default option or the way choices are presented, governments and organizations can guide people toward better decisions without restricting freedom of choice. For example, making organ donation opt-out rather than opt-in dramatically increases donation rates.

Understanding cognitive biases has practical implications across many fields. In medicine, awareness of biases can improve diagnostic accuracy. In finance, it can help investors make more rational decisions. In public policy, it can lead to more effective interventions. The challenge is that simply knowing about a bias does not necessarily prevent it from influencing behavior.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: Volunteering in Your Community
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
Volunteering is the act of giving your time and skills to help others without receiving payment. Millions of people around the world volunteer in their communities, contributing to a wide range of causes including education, healthcare, environmental protection, and social welfare.

The benefits of volunteering extend beyond the communities being helped. Research consistently shows that volunteers themselves gain significantly from the experience. Volunteering provides opportunities to develop new skills, build professional networks, and gain work experience. For young people, it can be particularly valuable when applying for jobs or university places.

Volunteering also has positive effects on mental health. Studies show that people who volunteer regularly report higher levels of happiness and life satisfaction. Helping others creates a sense of purpose and belonging, which can reduce feelings of loneliness and depression.

There are many ways to get involved in volunteering. Local charities and non-profit organizations are always looking for helpers. Schools and hospitals often welcome volunteers. Environmental groups organize regular clean-up events and conservation projects. Online volunteering has also grown, allowing people to contribute their skills remotely.

Despite its many benefits, volunteering rates have declined in some countries in recent years. Busy lifestyles, long working hours, and the demands of family life make it difficult for many people to find time to volunteer. Some organizations have responded by offering more flexible volunteering opportunities, such as micro-volunteering, which involves short tasks that can be completed in minutes.

Governments and employers can play a role in encouraging volunteering. Some companies offer paid volunteer days as part of their employee benefits. Schools can incorporate community service into their curriculum. Recognizing and celebrating volunteers publicly can also inspire others to get involved.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: The Sharing Economy
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
The sharing economy refers to a system where individuals share access to goods and services, typically facilitated by digital platforms. Companies like Airbnb, Uber, and TaskRabbit have built billion-dollar businesses by connecting people who have underutilized assets with those who need them.

The concept challenges traditional notions of ownership. Instead of buying a car, you can use a ride-sharing service. Instead of staying in a hotel, you can rent a room in someone home. Proponents argue that this model is more efficient, as it makes better use of existing resources and reduces waste.

The sharing economy has created new income opportunities for millions of people. Homeowners can earn money by renting spare rooms. Car owners can supplement their income by driving for ride-sharing platforms. Skilled individuals can offer services on freelance platforms. This flexibility appeals particularly to those seeking supplementary income or non-traditional work arrangements.

However, the sharing economy has also attracted significant criticism. Traditional businesses argue that sharing economy companies operate with unfair advantages, avoiding regulations that apply to hotels, taxis, and other established industries. Workers in the sharing economy are typically classified as independent contractors rather than employees, which means they lack benefits such as health insurance, paid leave, and job security.

Regulators around the world are grappling with how to govern sharing economy platforms. Some cities have banned or heavily restricted ride-sharing and home-sharing services to protect traditional industries and address concerns about housing affordability and safety.

The long-term impact of the sharing economy remains uncertain. While it has undoubtedly created value and convenience for many consumers, questions about worker rights, fair competition, and community impact continue to be debated.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Work
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
Artificial intelligence is transforming the global economy at an unprecedented pace. Unlike previous waves of automation, which primarily displaced manual and routine cognitive tasks, modern AI systems are increasingly capable of performing complex analytical, creative, and interpersonal functions that were previously considered uniquely human.

The economic implications are profound and contested. Optimists argue that AI will follow the historical pattern of technological disruption: while some jobs are eliminated, new categories of work emerge, productivity increases, and living standards rise. They point to the industrial revolution and the computerization of the 1980s and 1990s as precedents where fears of mass unemployment proved unfounded.

Pessimists counter that AI represents a qualitatively different challenge. Previous automation affected specific sectors, allowing workers to transition to other industries. AI, however, has the potential to automate across virtually all sectors simultaneously, leaving fewer safe harbors for displaced workers. Moreover, the pace of AI advancement may outstrip the ability of education and training systems to adapt.

The distributional effects of AI are also concerning. The benefits of AI-driven productivity gains are likely to accrue disproportionately to capital owners and highly skilled workers, potentially exacerbating existing inequalities. Workers in routine cognitive jobs, such as data entry, basic accounting, and customer service, face the greatest displacement risk.

Governments are beginning to grapple with these challenges. Proposals include universal basic income to provide a safety net for displaced workers, investment in retraining programs, and reforms to education systems to emphasize skills that are difficult to automate, such as critical thinking, creativity, and emotional intelligence.

The ultimate impact of AI on employment will depend on policy choices as much as technological capabilities. Societies that invest proactively in human capital and design inclusive institutions will be better positioned to share the benefits of AI while mitigating its disruptive effects.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: The Benefits of Exercise
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
Regular physical exercise is one of the most beneficial things a person can do for their health. Doctors and health organizations worldwide recommend at least 150 minutes of moderate exercise per week for adults. Despite this advice, many people lead sedentary lifestyles, spending most of their day sitting at desks or in front of screens.

The physical benefits of exercise are well-established. Regular activity strengthens the heart and improves cardiovascular health, reducing the risk of heart disease and stroke. It helps maintain a healthy weight by burning calories and boosting metabolism. Exercise also strengthens bones and muscles, reducing the risk of osteoporosis and injury as people age.

The mental health benefits are equally significant. Exercise releases endorphins, chemicals in the brain that create feelings of happiness and reduce stress. Studies show that regular exercise can be as effective as medication for treating mild to moderate depression and anxiety. It also improves sleep quality and cognitive function, including memory and concentration.

Despite these benefits, many people struggle to exercise regularly. Common barriers include lack of time, motivation, and access to facilities. However, exercise does not require expensive gym memberships or special equipment. Walking, cycling, swimming, and home workouts can all provide significant health benefits.

Building exercise into daily routines can help overcome these barriers. Taking the stairs instead of the lift, cycling to work, or going for a walk during lunch breaks are simple ways to increase physical activity. Setting realistic goals and tracking progress can also help maintain motivation.

The key is consistency. Even small amounts of regular exercise are better than occasional intense workouts. Starting slowly and gradually increasing intensity and duration is the safest and most sustainable approach for people who are new to exercise.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: Food Security in the 21st Century
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
Food security exists when all people, at all times, have physical and economic access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food to meet their dietary needs. Despite significant progress in reducing hunger over the past decades, food insecurity remains a major global challenge. According to the United Nations, approximately 800 million people worldwide are chronically undernourished.

Climate change poses an increasing threat to food security. Rising temperatures, changing rainfall patterns, and more frequent extreme weather events are disrupting agricultural production in many regions. Crop yields for staple foods such as wheat, rice, and maize are projected to decline in many areas as temperatures rise. Coastal and low-lying agricultural land is threatened by sea level rise and increased flooding.

Population growth adds further pressure. The global population is expected to reach approximately 10 billion by 2050, requiring a 50-70% increase in food production. This must be achieved while reducing the environmental impact of agriculture, which currently accounts for approximately 25% of global greenhouse gas emissions.

Technology offers potential solutions. Precision agriculture uses data and technology to optimize crop yields while minimizing resource use. Genetic modification and gene editing can produce crops that are more resistant to drought, pests, and disease. Vertical farming and controlled environment agriculture can produce food in urban areas with minimal land and water use.

Reducing food waste is another critical strategy. Approximately one-third of all food produced globally is lost or wasted. In developed countries, most waste occurs at the consumer level, while in developing countries, losses occur primarily during production and storage due to inadequate infrastructure.

Addressing food security requires coordinated action across multiple sectors. Agricultural investment, trade policy, social protection programs, and dietary change all have roles to play. Ensuring that smallholder farmers, who produce a significant proportion of the world food supply, have access to resources, technology, and markets is particularly important.

exam_version_reading_passages.title: Globalization and Cultural Identity
exam_version_reading_passages.passage:
Globalization, the process by which the world is becoming increasingly interconnected through trade, communication, and cultural exchange, has profoundly transformed societies around the world. While it has brought undeniable economic benefits and facilitated the spread of ideas, it has also raised complex questions about cultural identity and the preservation of local traditions.

Critics of globalization argue that it promotes cultural homogenization, the process by which diverse cultures become increasingly similar. The global dominance of American popular culture, including Hollywood films, fast food chains, and social media platforms, is often cited as evidence of this trend. Local languages, traditional arts, and indigenous practices face displacement as global culture permeates even the most remote communities.

Proponents counter that globalization does not necessarily destroy local cultures but can instead lead to cultural hybridization, the creative blending of global and local elements. Vietnamese hip-hop, Japanese-Italian fusion cuisine, and Bollywood films that incorporate Western musical elements are examples of how cultures adapt and innovate in response to global influences rather than simply being overwhelmed by them.

The relationship between globalization and cultural identity is further complicated by the digital revolution. Social media platforms simultaneously spread global culture and provide tools for marginalized communities to preserve and promote their languages and traditions. Indigenous communities in remote areas now use YouTube and Facebook to document and share their cultural practices with global audiences.

Economic inequality shapes how different communities experience globalization. Wealthy nations and individuals have greater power to shape global culture and to selectively adopt or reject foreign influences. Poorer communities often have less agency in this process and may face greater pressure to abandon traditional practices in favor of economically dominant cultural norms.

The challenge for societies navigating globalization is to find ways to participate in the global economy and culture while maintaining the diversity and distinctiveness that make human civilization rich. This requires conscious policy choices, including support for minority languages, cultural education, and the protection of intangible cultural heritage.

practice_writing_prompts.description: Viết thư xin lỗi vì đã quên sinh nhật.
practice_writing_prompts.prompt:
You forgot your close friend's birthday party last weekend. Write a letter to your friend to:
- Apologize for missing the party
- Explain why you couldn't attend
- Suggest a plan to make it up
practice_writing_prompts.sentence_starters: ["I am writing to...","I sincerely apologize for..."]
practice_writing_prompts.sample_answer:
Dear Minh,

I am writing to sincerely apologize for missing your birthday party last Saturday. I feel terrible about it.

The reason I couldn't attend was that my grandmother was suddenly hospitalized, and I had to rush to the hospital with my family. Everything happened so quickly that I didn't even have time to call you.

To make it up to you, I would like to invite you to dinner at your favorite restaurant this weekend. It will be my treat, and I promise to bring a special gift for you.

Once again, I am truly sorry. I hope you had a wonderful birthday despite my absence.

Best wishes,
Lan
